title: Noun gender — masculine, feminine, neuter
explanationMd: Every Russian noun has a grammatical gender, and the ending almost always gives it away in the dictionary form. **Masculine**: ends in a consonant (дом, друг) or й (чай). **Feminine**: ends in а/я (сестра, семья). **Neuter**: ends in о/е (молоко, метро). Gender matters constantly — adjectives, past-tense verbs, and pronouns all change to agree with it, so it's worth fixing in memory from the very first noun you learn, not something to patch in later.

examples.translation: This is a big house. (masculine agreement: большой)
This is a big family. (feminine agreement: большая)

commonErrors.note: семья is feminine (ends in я), so the adjective must take the feminine ending -ая, not the masculine большой.

drills.prompt: What gender is друг (based on its ending)?

title: Present tense has no 'to be'
explanationMd: Russian simply drops the present-tense 'to be' — there's no equivalent of 'am/is/are' to insert. Я студент means 'I [am a] student', word for word 'I student'. A dash (—) often stands in for it in writing when both sides are nouns: Москва — столица России ('Moscow is the capital of Russia'). This isn't an abbreviation of anything; the verb genuinely isn't there in the present tense (it reappears as был/была/было in the past and будет in the future).

commonErrors.wrong: Я есть студент. (inserting есть as if it were 'am')
commonErrors.note: есть as an existential 'there is' survives in a few fixed patterns (У меня есть...), but not as a general-purpose 'to be' linking a subject to a noun/adjective.

title: The six-case system — what a 'case' actually does
explanationMd: Russian nouns, pronouns, and adjectives change their endings depending on their grammatical role in the sentence — this is the case system, and it's the single biggest structural difference from English. There are six cases: **nominative** (the subject, dictionary form), **genitive** (possession/negation, roughly 'of'), **dative** (indirect object, roughly 'to'), **accusative** (direct object), **instrumental** (the means, roughly 'by/with'), and **prepositional** (location, always after certain prepositions). Word order in Russian is flexible precisely because the case endings — not position in the sentence — mark who's doing what to whom. This pack introduces the prepositional (for location) and genitive (for negation) first, since they carry the most everyday weight; the rest are follow-up work.

examples.translation: This is Anna. (nominative — the base/dictionary form)
Anna has a brother. (genitive: Анны, not Анна)

commonErrors.wrong: У Анна есть брат. (leaving the name in nominative)
commonErrors.note: After у ('by/at', used for possession), the noun must switch to genitive — nominative Анна isn't grammatical here.

drills.prompt: How many grammatical cases does Russian have?
drills.answer: Six: nominative, genitive, dative, accusative, instrumental, prepositional.

title: Prepositional case — saying where something is
explanationMd: To say something is located somewhere, use **в** ('in') or **на** ('on/at') plus the noun in the **prepositional case** — for most nouns, that means adding -е to the stem. дом → в доме́ ('in the house'), Москва → в Москве́ ('in Moscow'), работа → на рабо́те ('at work'). This case is called 'prepositional' precisely because it only ever appears after a preposition — в and на are its two most common triggers at this level. в generally means inside an enclosed space; на is for surfaces, events, and a set list of exceptions (на работе, на почте) that are best memorized as vocabulary.

commonErrors.note: The noun after в must take the prepositional ending -е — leaving it in nominative (Москва) is a common beginner slip.

title: Genitive case — negating 'there is' with нет
explanationMd: 'There isn't/aren't any' uses **нет** plus the noun in the **genitive case** — the mirror image of the affirmative у меня есть pattern from the case-system overview. У меня есть брат ('I have a brother') negates to У меня нет бра́та ('I don't have a brother') — брат becomes бра́та, not just tacking нет onto the nominative. This genitive-after-negation pattern is extremely common and worth internalizing early, since it comes up constantly in daily complaints and simple statements alike.

commonErrors.note: нет requires the following noun in genitive — брат must become брата, the same way Анна became Анны after у.

title: First conjugation present tense (-е/-ю verbs)
explanationMd: Most Russian verbs fall into one of two present-tense patterns. First-conjugation verbs (infinitives typically ending in -ать/-ять, like знать and читать) use these endings: я -ю/-у, ты -ешь, он/она -ет, мы -ем, вы -ете, они -ют/-ут. знать ('to know') conjugates: я зна́ю, ты зна́ешь, он зна́ет, мы зна́ем, вы зна́ете, они зна́ют. The stem (зна-) stays constant — only the ending changes, which makes this pattern genuinely predictable once you've seen it a few times.

commonErrors.note: -ю is the я-ending only; the он/она-form always takes -ет in first conjugation.

title: Second conjugation present tense (-и verbs)
explanationMd: Second-conjugation verbs (infinitives typically ending in -ить, like говорить) use a different ending set: я -ю/-у, ты -ишь, он/она -ит, мы -им, вы -ите, они -ят/-ат. говорить conjugates: я говорю́, ты говори́шь, он говори́т, мы говори́м, вы говори́те, они говоря́т. The я and они forms often look similar to first conjugation at a glance, but the ты/он/мы/вы middle forms (-ишь/-ит/-им/-ите) are the reliable tell for which pattern a verb follows.

commonErrors.note: Second conjugation он/она-forms take -ит, not the first-conjugation -ет.

title: Adjective agreement with noun gender
explanationMd: An adjective's ending must match the gender (and later, case) of the noun it describes. The dictionary/masculine form typically ends in -ый/-ий/-ой; feminine swaps to -ая/-яя; neuter to -ое/-ее. большой дом (m), большая семья (f), — there's no neuter example among this pack's nouns yet, but the pattern is большое молоко-type endings for neuter nouns. Get in the habit of checking a noun's gender before reaching for an adjective ending, the same reflex as checking subject-verb agreement in English.

commonErrors.note: работа is feminine, so the adjective needs the feminine ending -ая, not masculine -ий.

title: Yes/no questions — intonation, not word order
explanationMd: Russian doesn't invert word order or add a helper word to turn a statement into a yes/no question the way English does with 'do/does'. Ты говоришь по-русски ('you speak Russian') becomes a question purely through rising intonation: Ты говоришь по-русски? ('do you speak Russian?'). In writing, only the question mark shows it changed. Question-word questions (что, где, кто, когда, как, почему) go at the front of the sentence, much like English, and don't need any additional marker either.

commonErrors.wrong: Do you speak Russian? → До ты говоришь по-русски? (inventing a 'do' word)
commonErrors.note: There's no equivalent of English 'do' to insert — rising intonation alone marks the question.

drills.prompt: Turn into a question: Она понимает.
